texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas separation membranes can be applied to a range of processing steps during natural gas treatment. This paper considers the current and future potential of polymeric membranes in acidic gas removal, heavy hydrocarbon recovery, water dehydration as well as nitrogen and helium separation. In particular, the current commercial membrane materials for CO2 removal (cellulose acetate, polyimides and perfluoropolymers) are reviewed, along with membrane process design and the conditions required to make membrane processes economically competitive with other technologies. The current application of membranes for heavy hydrocarbon recovery is also investigated. Issues affecting membrane viability are explored, such as competitive sorption, plasticization and the influence of module design on performance. In addition, for each application, comments are provided on the future progress of membranes in these areas. Membranes for dehydration, nitrogen separation and helium recovery are less developed, so the current potential for membranes in those applications are reviewed with a focus on the direction of future research to achieve commercially viable processes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membranes for natural gas processing were first commercialized in the 1980s for CO2 removal [5,6], and have been the dominant membrane gas separation process since then. There are also other processing stages in natural gas treatment that can utilize membranes, and these are also now being commercially deployed. In particular, the removal and recovery of heavier hydrocarbons from the raw gas, dehydration, as well as separation of nitrogen and other inert gases is possible [7]. These processes are undertaken to produce natural gas at the necessary composition for consumption, as well as to recover valuable components. A good example of this is the recovery of heavier hydrocarbons, such as butane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In all of these situations, polymeric membranes are the focus of commercialization because of their ease of manufacture [8]. Other materials, such as inorganic membranes, demonstrate high performance under research conditions but module fabrication is still too expensive to compete against current polymeric modules. However, it is worthwhile noting that only a small number of polymers of the many hundreds developed in the research laboratory have themselves reached their commercial potential. In 2002, only nine polymeric materials made up 90% of the total gas separation membrane market [9].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polymeric membranes are generally non-porous and gas permeation is described by the solution diffusion mechanism. This is based on the solubility of specific gases within the membrane and their diffusion through the dense membrane matrix. Hence, separation is not just dependent upon molecular size but also relies on the chemical interaction between the gases and the polymer. Polymeric membranes are further classified as rubbery or glassy dependent on the value of their glass transition temperature relative to ambient. Rubbery membranes selectivity is generally solubility based while glassy membrane selectivity is generally diffusion based.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here, the application of polymeric membranes to natural gas processing is reviewed based on current academic literature and patent examples. In particular, the focus is on membranes applied to acidic gas separation (CO2 and H2S), heavy hydrocarbon removal, water removal, N2 removal as well as Helium recovery. The review covers available membrane materials, their performance and limitations, processing strategies, as well as an analysis of future trends in each processing stage. The performance of each membrane is reported either as the pressure normalized flux or permeance of a gas species in GPU ( -10 � 6 cm 3 (STP)/cm 2 s cmHg), or when the thickness of the membrane is known, as a permeability, which is quoted in Barrer (10 � 10 cm 3 (STP) cm/cm 2 s cmHg).

texts.orig: 2. Acidic gas separation
texts.text: 2. Acidic gas separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The widest application for gas separation membranes is the removal of acidic gases, CO2 and H2S, from raw natural gas. Almost 20% of natural gas fields in the USA require acidic gas removal [2], and it will become more common in the future as low-quality reserves with high CO2 and H2S content become economically viable. Current installations can process up to 680 million standard cubic feet per day (MMscfd) while projects of up to 1200 MMscfd are currently being developed [10]. CO2 inlet concentrations range from 5% to 70% [11].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are three main types of membranes commercially available for CO2 removal; cellulose acetate, polyimides and perfluoropolymers. The details of each will be discussed individually below, along with other potential polymers. In addition to their gas permeation properties, these polymers are all easy to process through to membrane modules, are mechanically strong and have thermal and chemical resistance to ensure long term viability [12].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Cellulose acetate
texts.text: 2.1. Cellulose acetate

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Cellulose acetate based membranes for CO2 separation from CH4 have been commercial since the mid-1980s, and arose directly from the use of cellulose acetate in reverse osmosis membranes [13]. Generally, the membranes consist of a combination of cellulose acetate, cellulose diacetate and/or cellulose triacetate. These membranes make up to 80% of the market for membranes in natural gas processing, and because of their wide industrial acceptance have become an industry standard for comparison purposes. There are two major membrane manufacturers currently supplying cellulose acetate based modules; these are Cynara Ò (part of Cameron) and UOP Separex Ò (part of Honeywell). The Cynara Ò systems are based on asymmetric hollow fibers [10], while Separex Ò uses a spiral wound module. The major market for cellulose acetate membranes are for high CO2 fields and where space on site is limited, such as offshore platforms. For large scale processes it has been estimated that cellulose acetate is only economically competitive with solvent absorption when the natural gas contains over 10-20 mol% CO2, depending on the wellhead pressure [14].

texts.orig: The other critical factor is fabrication of the membrane, and importantly the approach taken to achieve a dry film [17-24]. This is important because the evaporation of water from the drying membrane leads to capillary forces collapsing the microporous structure, creating a denser active layer [13]. Approaches such as freeze drying and solvent exchange can be used. A number of these drying approaches were patented in the mid 1970s [25-27]. In the case of solvent exchange, water, a high surface tension solvent, is gradually replaced by a low surface tension solvent that has less impact on the membrane structure when evaporated. The effect of different solvents in the exchange on the membrane performance is shown in Table 2. Each of the drying solvents influences the underlying polymer morphology differently; however there is no clear trend in terms of surface tension and gas permeation properties. For example, drying with heptane results in a very low permeability and poor selectivity compared with decane or even xylene, even though both of these solvents have higher surface tension. Also, there is not a clear distinction that reduced permeance will enhance or reduce selectivity, with heptane, toluene and cycloheptane all producing reduced permeability and selectivity, while xylene and decane have reduced permeance but good selectivity. The evaporation temperature is also critical, with Minhas et al. [24] showing that  80 ° C provides the best morphology for selectivity but generally a low permeability, dependent on solvent combinations.

texts.orig: effects and resulting in catastrophic membrane failure [28,29]. Houde et al. [30] observes a CO2 plasticization pressure of  10 atm for a dense membrane of 76 l m thickness, while Donohue et al. [31] observes plasticization at <5 atm for asymmetric cellulose acetate membranes. Lower plasticization pressures in thinner membranes is a well known phenomenon, especially for asymmetric membranes [32]. Some studies have shown no change in CO2 plasticization pressure with different degree of acetylation [33]. However, other hysteresis studies have shown that CO2 alters the morphology of a higher acetylated membrane more strongly, which is attributed to the higher intrinsic solubility of CO2 and the greater number of acetyl-acetyl interactions present. These are weaker compared to the hydroxyl-hydroxyl or -acetyl interactions and therefore reduce the intermolecular network holding the polymer chains together. Some studies take advantage of plasticization, known as ''conditioning'', to increase the fractional free volume of the membrane during fabrication [34]. Plasticizing the membrane with CO2 alters the voidage space between cellulose acetate chains to accommodate more gas, and when CO2 is removed, the long-time frame of polymer mobility means the membrane remains in this altered state [35,36]. The effects of conditioning are time dependent, with the longer the membrane exposed to high pressure CO2 the more pronounced the effects. Evidence of these effects is provided in Table 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As with all gas separation membrane materials, the performance of cellulose acetate is also reduced under mixed gas conditions due to competitive sorption for the Langmuir void spaces [37]. Houde et al. [30] found that in a mixture of methane with either 9.7 or 24 vol% CO2 the permeability of both gases decline with increasing pressure, due to competitive sorption effects. Conversely, in a mixture of 46.1 vol% CO2, the permeability initially declines as pressure is increased, due to competitive sorption, but increases again at higher total pressures, due to plasticization. Under all conditions, membrane selectivity falls for the gas mixture relative to the pure gas, as both competitive sorption and plasticization causes such a decline (Fig. 2). Donohue et al. observes similar results [31].

Removing these components from the natural gas will increase the lifetime of the modules. However, cellulose acetate membranes are resilient to aromatic hydrocarbons such as benzene and xylene, often found in natural gas [6].

This is because cellulose acetate is cheap and fabrication methods are well known, and can reduce the cost of fabrication for an expensive novel polymer because the expensive polymer is limited to only the thin active layer.

texts.orig: There is little likelihood that novel cellulose acetate based membranes in the future will have improved performance upon those currently commercialized. While a wide range of cellulose derivative polymers have been created, these only achieve small improvements in separation performance or increased resilience to CO2 plasticization and chemical degradation. Unless a significant breakthrough occurs, it is expected that research into cellulose acetate as a gas separation membrane will reduce, as more attractive polymers come into focus.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Polyimides
texts.text: 2.2. Polyimides

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polyimides are a class of polymer that display high permeability for CO2 and good selectivity against CH4. They are viewed as an alternative to cellulose acetate, because they are also easy to prepare as asymmetric membranes while demonstrating good thermal and chemical stability [55]. Polyimides consist of diamine-diahydride segments that have undergone polycondensation [56], and because of this a wide range of polyimides exist with good gas separation properties based on varying both the diamine and dianhydride. A range of polyimide membranes are given in Table 5 with the most common reported in laboratory studies being Matrimid 5218. Polyimide membranes have been commercialized by UBE Industries while Air Liquide's Medal membrane consists of polyimide and polyaramides. These polyimide membranes are usually formatted into hollow fiber designs [57], which can achieve three times the area per unit volume as spiral wound modules [8].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The generally high permeability and selectivity of polyimides makes them more attractive as a membrane compared to cellulose acetate. Baker [9] provides an example for standard natural gas processing where a polyimide membrane in place of a cellulose acetate membrane results in an area reduction of  40% and recycle compressor duty reduction of  35%. Furthermore, for the same process design the methane lost in the permeate stream is reduced by 75% for a polyimide membrane compared to cellulose acetate. However, there have been concerns about the performance of these membranes in the presence of gas impurities with performance under controlled laboratory conditions often failing to be achieved under real conditions. More expensive pretreatment processes are often required to ensure adequate membrane life. White [13] has argued that the separation performance loss between pure gas measurements and actual practice is greater than that which occurs for cellulose acetate, and it is for this reason that cellulose acetate retains its market share. The mixed gas results for Matrimid 5218, provided in Table 6, support this by showing a reduction of 33% in CO2 permeability and 20% in CO2/CH4 selectivity at 7.5 bar feed pressure, while cellulose acetate experiences much smaller losses at 10 atm feed pressure (Fig. 2). The reduced performance in polyimide membranes under mixed gas conditions is again primarily due to competitive sorption from other gases. The smaller market share of polyimide membranes is also associated with the fact they are considerably more expensive and were not commercialized until a decade later than cellulose acetate,

texts.orig: Similar to cellulose acetate, polyimides are known to be susceptible to plasticization by CO2, for example dense 6FDA-DAD at 35 ° C is plasticized by 14 bar CO2 [68], while for asymmetric Matrimid the plasticization pressure is <4 bar [69]. Again, the plasticization pressure is thickness dependent [70]. There is evidence that polyimides are more sensitive to plasticization than cellulose acetate [71,72], and it is postulated this is because of the more ordered structure in the polyimide film, compared to cellulose acetate, making it more susceptible to structural changes [73].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polyimides are also impacted by minor components in the natural gas. UBE's polyimide membrane has been reported to be affected by ammonia and aromatic hydrocarbons at low concentrations [8]. Similarly White [13] provides evidence that hexane plasticizes polyimide membranes and naphthalene fouls the polyimide membrane surface. Various approaches have been undertaken to improve polyimide performance, in terms of gas separation properties, as well as resistance to CO2 plasticization and other components in natural gas. Approaches such as hyperbranching and cross-linking [74-77], mixed matrix membranes [78-81] and blending with other polymers, such as polysulfone and polyethersulfone [69,82], have been trialed. For example, Bos et al. [83] reports an increase in the plasticization pressure of Matrimid when blended with Thermid FA-700 from  10 bar to  20 bar.

texts.orig: The sheer number of polyimides that have been trialed for gas separation means this class of polymer will remain an active membrane research area for some time to come. It is highly probably that within the coming decade a number of modified polyimides, especially mixed matrix and blends, will be commercialized for natural gas processing. It is expected that polyimide gas separation membranes will increase their market share over time as their versatility and competitiveness against cellulose acetate becomes more apparent.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Perfluoropolymers
texts.text: 2.3. Perfluoropolymers

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Perfluoropolymers are a class of polymers that display unique resistance to hostile chemical and thermal environments [84], and in particular resistance to plasticization from both CO2 and hydrocarbons [85]. This has made them attractive for natural gas processing, as they are not susceptible to the same problems as polyimides and cellulose acetate. The reason for their unique properties is the high energy of the C-F bonds that exists in the substituent groups off the polymer backbone. The most common perfluoropolymer is poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE). However, PTFE and similar polymers, such as poly (hexafluoropropylene-tetrafluoroethylene), have poor gas separation properties because of a high degrees of crystallinity when cast as films. The discovery in the 1980s of amorphous perfluoropolymers, in particular Cytop Ó , Teflon AF Ó and Hyflon AD Ó [86,87], that utilize bulky fluorine substituent groups to inhibit chain packing has enabled efficient gas separation membranes to be fabricated. The permeability and selectivity performance of some common perfluoropolymers are listed in Table 7. Another advantage of perfluoropolymers is their processability for membrane manufacture, arising from their high solubility in fluorinated solvents and subsequent low solution viscosities. Membrane Technology and Research Inc. (MTR) is commercializing perfluoropolymer membranes for natural gas processing, under the name Z-Top.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The perfluoropolymers exhibit ultrahigh free volume for glassy polymers, especially Teflon AF2400 [84] which is only exceeded by poly(1-trimethylsilyl-1-propyne) (PTMSP). However, as with most glassy polymeric membranes, the high CO2 permeability has a trade-off in terms of low CO2/CH4 selectivity, with few perfluoropolymers achieving the types of selectivities observed for polyimides. Importantly, the plasticization pressure of CO2 is very high for this class of polymers, with both Teflon AD2400 [84,88] and Hyflon AD80 [89] displaying almost no evidence of plasticization up to 20 bar CO2. In addition, perfluoropolymers have excellent organophobicity and hydrophobicity, as demonstrated in Table 8, based on their contact angle for water and hexadecane. These high angles are indicative of poor wetting properties and therefore reduced ability of both water and hydrocarbons to plasticize perfluoropolymer membranes. This makes perfluoropolymers attractive for low-quality natural gas wells, where significant amounts of water and/or heavy hydrocarbons would rapidly degrade other membranes.

Here, solution casting provides high permeabilities, partly because some solvent remains present within the fractional free volume that facilitates gas transport, but melt-pressing, with reduced solvent, generates the greater selectivity [93].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.4. Other polymers
texts.text: 2.4. Other polymers

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A range of other polymers show potential for acidic gas removal from natural gas, however to date none have been commercialized. Polybenzoxzoles (PBOs) are a class of polymers with good gas permeation and selectivity properties that show promise [100]. In particular, those PBOs generated by the thermal-rearrangement approach are insoluble in almost every solvent and are therefore of interest from a plasticization resistance perspective [101,102]. However, the membrane material can be brittle and a fabrication methodology will need to be resolved before these polymers can be commercialized. Facilitated transport membranes, based on a facilitator species transferring chemically bonded CO2 across an immobilized liquid membrane, have excellent permeability properties and can achieve selectivities orders of magnitude greater than observed in standard polymers [103]. However, the immobilized liquid layer is generally water based, so issues such as water evaporation and hydrocarbon fouling will need to be addressed. An active research area to overcome these issues is to base the membrane on ionic liquids.

However, given that gas separation membranes have been commercialized for three decades and only a few polymers are commercially successful, it is expected that very few of the polymeric membranes under research will ever make it to the commercial scale.

texts.orig: 2.5. Plant design
texts.text: 2.5. Plant design

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The method of membrane fabrication can have considerable influence on performance and there are a range of techniques available; such as melt-pressing, solution casting, phase inversion, sputtering, extruding and interfacial polymerization [104-115]. Membranes can be fabricated either in a hollow fiber or spiral wound format. The spiral wound format was the first to be commercialized and replicates the format used in many water treatment processes. In this case, two flat sheet membranes are sealed together with a spacer placed both between the sheets and on one external side. The sandwiched sheets are then rolled into a spiral format around a central permeate collection channel. In this modular design the permeate flow is cross-flow to the feed, an arrangement which is less thermodynamically efficient than countercurrent flow. However, the relatively wide permeate channels ensure a low pressure drop and the feed side spacers act to increase turbulence, thus reducing concentration polarization. Conversely, the feed spacers can trap condensed liquids [116], leading to performance decline caused by plasticization. The arrangement can also be easily damaged by compaction if high differential pressures (>125 bar) are utilized.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: More recently hollow fiber membrane modules have been developed. These modules contain thousands of fibers, each typically 0.1-0.5 mm in diameter, within a shell. A major advantage of this approach is a much greater membrane area per unit volume, which can be important where the process footprint is limited, such as in offshore operations. The approach also allows greater flexibility in operation, as feed can be placed on either the lumen or shell side and counter-current, co-current and cross-flow are all possible. Fabrication costs for hollow fiber spinning are considerably lower at $2-5 m 2 compared to spiral wound module fabrication at $10-100 per m 2 [9].

texts.orig: A range of process designs exist [118], with a standard twostage membrane process design provided in Fig. 4. In this design, the first membrane unit separates CO2 from the gas to achieve pipeline specification. However, because methane also permeates through the membrane, losses of  12% are observed. Therefore, the permeate stream from the first membrane unit is compressed and passed through a second membrane unit to recover the CH4. This is operated at a low stagecut to minimize CH4 losses through the membrane to 1.5%. The retentate from the second membrane unit, which has a significant amount of CO2 present, is recycled to the feed of the first membrane unit. Yang et al. [119] showed that for such a two stage process, applied to reduce a natural gas stream from 10 vol% to 2 vol% CO2, the compression energy was only 107.5 kJ/m 3 (STP) which compared very favorably with the energy consumption of an amine absorption process of 600900 kJ/m 3 (STP) even considering the conversion factor of 3-4 between heat and electricity. More complex processes are possible, especially when significant amounts of heavy hydrocarbons are present, along with H2S, that can be separated together or in stages and then isolated [120].

texts.orig: Raw natural gas is often saturated with propane, butane and higher hydrocarbons, which need to be separated before the gas can be piped. This is to prevent formation of hydrocarbon liquids in the piping (dew point specification is � 20 ° C). Further, these heavier hydrocarbons are often of greater value when sold separately. The current separation technology of choice is a sequence of cooling and condensation ( � 40 ° C), or lean oil absorption. The separated hydrocarbons are subsequently separated by fractional distillation [2].

texts.orig: Membranes can be competitive in this processing application. Rubbery polymeric membranes are the focus, because of their ability to separate based on gas condensability. In this case, heavy hydrocarbons are able to permeate at a greater flux than methane. Some example of rubbery polymeric membranes for heavy hydrocarbon separation are provided in Table 10, where the selectivity for butane/methane separation is provided based on pure and mixed gas results. Other possible rubbery membranes are nitrile rubber, neoprene, chlorosulfonated polyethylene, polysilicone-carbonate, polyvinylchloride, polyurethane, polybutadiene, polyisoprene, polyether, and polyesters, as well as copolymer combinations of these. As is common in membrane systems, there is a reduction in membrane performance under mixed gas conditions compared to pure gas results, in this case because of unfavorable interactions between gases within the rubbery polymeric matrix.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Silicone rubber, or polydimethyl siloxane (PDMS), is the most recognized rubbery membrane for this application because it is cheap, readily available, has some of the highest permeabilities reported and reasonable selectivities (Table 11) for methane relative to other heavy hydrocarbons [122]. However, PDMS itself has selectivities in general that are inadequate to achieve good separation. Therefore, to achieve significant recovery of heavy hydrocarbons while minimizing methane loses, a number of membrane stages and recycle streams are required. This makes processes based on PDMS inefficient for treating natural gas. Further, heavy hydrocarbons are known to swell the PDMS structure and alter its gas separation ability. This is shown in Fig. 5, where 10 days of butane exposure leads to a dramatic increase in permeance compared to nitrogen and even propane.

texts.orig: Glassy polymeric membranes are unlikely to be commercial for hydrocarbon removal, because they are in general diffusivity selective. Therefore, the bulk of the natural gas, methane, would have to permeate leaving the heavier hydrocarbons in the retentate stream. Subsequently, this would require very large membrane areas, and also considerable compressor duty to repressurize the majority of the natural gas. However, the glassy polymer poly (1trimethylsilyl-1-propyne) (PTMSP) has demonstrated remarkable abilities to separate like a rubbery membrane, in that heavy hydrocarbons permeate at a greater rate than methane [125].

texts.orig: The process design for removing heavy hydrocarbons from raw natural gas is relatively simple, and shown in Fig. 6. The standard design is for a single membrane separation step to recover and concentrate the majority of the heavy hydrocarbons into the permeate stream. This stream is then compressed and a simple condenser stage is used to remove the heavy hydrocarbons from the methane that passed through the membrane. The methane is then recirculated to the feed. This design can be modified [129] to take into account the fact that the vapor/liquid equilibrium in the condensation stage might lead to a high heavy hydrocarbon concentration being recirculated to the feed stream, and therefore the need for an additional membrane stage to reduce this is included.

texts.orig: The biggest drawback for membranes in this application is the amount of methane lost with the water permeating the membrane. Even though the membrane may have very high selectivity, a significant amount of methane permeates the membrane due to pressure ratio limitations [2,9,131]. The partial pressure of water in the feed must be greater than that in the permeate stream, hence the separation achieved is always smaller than the pressure ratio, regardless of membrane selectivity. Membrane units used to dry air also experience this problem [131]. In natural gas processing a loss of just 1% methane makes the process noncompetitive. Baker [2] has shown that even for a H2O/CH4 selectivity of 500 a pressure ratio across of the membrane of >800 is required to limit methane loss in the permeate stream to under 1%. To achieve this pressure ratio Baker [2] postulates that a membrane system using vacuum on the permeate side could be competitive with glycol dehydration in offshore applications, because the membrane system will be one-fifth the weight and footprint of a glycol absorption plant. Alternatively, the use of permeate compression and subsequent removal of liquid water, with the methane being recycled to the feed, has been postulated.

This is because current commercial practices are too well established and cheap for membranes to compete in all but the most marginal situations. Rather, research and development on process designs, especially hybrid systems, is needed to generate a competitive process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Significant volumes of natural gas are considered low quality because of the large nitrogen content, generally well above the <4% pipeline specification. Removal of this nitrogen could allow access to an estimated 10 trillion scf additional natural gas in the USA alone [9]. To date N2 separation has been achieved through cryogenic distillation, which also has the added benefit of removing many of the heavier hydrocarbons. However, cryogenic natural gas-nitrogen separation plants are capital intensive, and must be very large to take advantage of economy of scale. Hence, this approach is more suited to large fields that can deliver 50-500 million scf per day [138]. Membrane-based nitrogen separation has a potential market in smaller natural gas operations, where cryogenic distillation is uneconomical. Lokhandwala et al. [138] indicate that twelve membrane-based nitrogen removal systems had been installed by 2010 using this approach.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Natural gas is a considerable source of helium, especially in locations in the USA. A large installation has also recently been installed in northern Australia, which will produce 900 tonnes a year of helium from nitrogen reject gases of an LNG facility [145]. Currently, helium is removed with nitrogen by cryogenic distillation [2] and isolated in a separate cryogenic process. However, given the marginal economics of cryogenic distillation, membrane separation for helium has been considered since the 1960s [146], with cellulose acetate the first material trialed [147]. The permeability and selectivity of a range of polymeric membranes to helium is provided in Table 15 and the Robeson's plot in Fig. 9. A significant problem is that the majority of reservoirs have very low He concentrations, and therefore to achieve good separation of He with minimal CH4 loss, very high He selectivies are required (1000 against CH4) [7]. Alternatively multi-membrane cascade processes [148,149] or hybrid systems with other separation technologies [150-153] have been considered. These processes have limitations, such as the considerable energy penalty because of permeate recompression.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7. Conclusion
texts.text: 7. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas separation can be applied to a range of processing operations for the treatment of natural gas. In particular acidic gas removal (e.g. CO2) and heavy hydrocarbon recovery are currently commercialized and show the greatest potential for growth over the coming decade. This is because the polymeric membranes utilized in these applications have been successfully proven and efficient processes have been implemented. Membrane gas separation will grow in this market as the technology is increasing seen as commercially competitive and less high risk. The relatively small footprint and weight of membrane systems mean that they will become the technology of choice for offshore and on-ship processing operations; while their ease of operation means that they can be readily applied in remote locations with minimal on-site labor. The gas fields of the future will be of lower quality with higher CO2 and H2S content and this also lends itself to the use of membrane technology for gas sweetening.
texts.text: Membrane gas separation can be applied to a range of processing operations for the treatment of natural gas. In particular acidic gas removal (e.g. CO2) and heavy hydrocarbon recovery are currently commercialized and show the greatest potential for growth over the coming decade. This is because the polymeric membranes utilized in these applications have been successfully proven and efficient processes have been implemented. Membrane gas separation will grow in this market as the technology is increasing seen as commercially competitive and less high risk. The relatively small footprint and weight of membrane systems mean that they will become the technology of choice for offshore and on-ship processing operations; while their ease of operation means that they can be readily applied in remote locations with minimal on-site labor. The gas fields of the future will be of lower quality with higher CO2 and H2S content and this also lends itself to the use of membrane technology for gas sweetening.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Other process applications, such as N2 and helium gas removal are likely to develop more slowly as the membranes available for these applications do not yet show sufficient selectivity. Conversely, in membrane dehydration, current membrane selectivity is more than adequate, but some methane slip cannot be avoided due to pressure ratio limitations and this restricts the ability of membranes to compete with glycol absorption technology.
